message:
fix(runtime): refine stuck-task heuristic, report truthful worker storage capabilities, skip stale scheduler entries

Task inspector (task_inspector.rs):
- Extract is_potentially_stuck() method on TaskDetails. The old
  find_stuck_tasks used age > threshold alone, which flagged every
  long-lived waiting task (timers, idle connections) as "stuck" when
  they were legitimately parked. The new heuristic:
  1. Never flags terminal or wake-pending tasks.
  2. When time_since_last_poll metadata is available, uses it directly.
  3. When last-poll metadata is unavailable (runtime doesn't track
     wall-clock polls yet), only flags aged tasks with poll_count == 0,
     avoiding false positives on tasks that have been polled but are
     simply waiting.
- summary() now uses the same is_potentially_stuck method for
  consistency.
- Added tests: old polled task without idle metadata is not flagged,
  old polled task with idle metadata above threshold is flagged,
  inspector integration test confirms no false positives.

Browser execution probe (builder.rs):
- Extract browser_storage_capabilities_for_host_role() const fn that
  returns truthful storage capabilities per host role:
  - BrowserMainThread: IndexedDB + localStorage
  - DedicatedWorker/ServiceWorker/SharedWorker: IndexedDB only (workers
    have IndexedDB access but not localStorage per the Web spec)
  - NonBrowserOrUnknown: neither
  Previously all probes returned has_indexed_db=false, which made
  diagnostics falsely report that workers lack IndexedDB, confusing
  users who see "no storage available" in their execution ladder.
- All four host-role probes now use the helper instead of inline
  struct literals.
- Added test assertions verifying storage capabilities per host role.

Priority scheduler (priority.rs):
- steal_work now skips stale entries (tasks already removed from the
  scheduled set by cancel/remove) instead of counting them toward
  the steal budget. Previously a stale entry would consume one of the
  N steal slots, returning fewer than N tasks even when valid tasks
  were available — starving idle workers in high-cancellation
  workloads.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
